en.extract:
The spot-breasted oriole (Icterus pectoralis) is a species of bird in the family Icteridae.
It is a mid-sized songbird and generally typical oriole. It is bright orange overall with a black bib and black spotting on the sides of the breast. The sexes are similar looking generally but females and juveniles are olive-green on the back and tail, dusky wings, and little or no black on face, throat, or breast. Adults measure 21–24 cm (8.3–9.4 in) in length. Males weigh around 50 g (1.8 oz) on average, while females weigh 45 g (1.6 oz). The wing bone measures 8.8–11.4 cm (3.5–4.5 in), the tail measures 8.5–11.2 cm (3.3–4.4 in), the culmen measures 1.9–2.4 cm (0.75–0.94 in) and the tarsus measures 2.6–3 cm (1.0–1.2 in).
It is native to Costa Rica, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, and Nicaragua.
Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests, subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, and heavily degraded former forest.
The spot-breasted oriole ranges only on the Pacific side of Central America. An introduced breeding population also exists on the Atlantic coast of southern Florida. The population is considered to be established enough to be "countable" for birdwatchers by the ABA.

The '''spot-breasted oriole''' ('''''Icterus pectoralis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Icteridae]].

It is a mid-sized songbird and generally typical [[New World oriole|oriole]]. It is bright orange overall with a black bib and black spotting on the sides of the breast. The sexes are similar looking generally but females and juveniles are olive-green on the back and tail, dusky wings, and little or no black on face, throat, or breast.<ref name= Cornell>{{Cite web|url=http://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Spot-breasted_Oriole/lifehistory|title = Spot-breasted Oriole Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology}}</ref> Adults measure {{convert|21|-|24|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length. Males weigh around {{convert|50|g|oz|abbr=on}} on average, while females weigh {{convert|45|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Cornell/><ref>''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0849342585}}.</ref> The wing bone measures {{convert|8.8|-|11.4|cm|in|abbr=on}}, the tail measures {{convert|8.5|-|11.2|cm|in|abbr=on}}, the [[Culmen (bird)|culmen]] measures {{convert|1.9|-|2.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} and the [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] measures {{convert|2.6|-|3|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>''New World Blackbirds: The Icterids'' by Alvaro Jaramillo & Peter Burke. Christopher Helm Publishing (1999), {{ISBN|978-0713643336}}</ref>

It is native to [[Costa Rica]], [[El Salvador]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], [[Mexico]], and [[Nicaragua]].

Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical dry [[forest]]s, subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests, and heavily degraded former forest.

The spot-breasted oriole ranges only on the [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]] side of [[Central America]]. An introduced breeding population also exists on the [[Atlantic Ocean|Atlantic]] coast of southern [[Florida]]. The population is considered to be established enough to be "countable" for birdwatchers by the [[American Birding Association|ABA]].<ref name= Cornell/>
